title: Part I: Oblomov's Day
paragraphs: In Gorokhovaya Street, in one of the large houses whose population was large enough to constitute a small town, Ilya Ilyich Oblomov lay in bed one morning.

He was a man of about two-and-thirty, of medium height, and of pleasant appearance. Unfortunately, in his dark grey eyes there was an absence of any definite idea, and in the rest of his features a total lack of concentration. Suddenly a thought wandered across his face like a bird in flight; it fluttered in his eyes, settled on his half-opened lips, and hid itself in the wrinkles of his forehead. Then it disappeared utterly, and his face resumed its vacant and carefree expression.

Oblomov's dressing-gown was made of Persian material and was a genuine Oriental robe—without the faintest suggestion of Europe about it. No tassels, no velvet, no waist. It was spacious and large enough for him to wrap himself round in it twice. The sleeves, true to the Asiatic fashion, gradually widened from the wrists to the shoulders.
Although the dressing-gown had lost its original freshness and here and there showed shiny, threadbare patches, it still retained its Oriental brilliance of colour and the soundness of its fabric.
Oblomov treasured it as a relic of the past; he liked lying in it on his sofa, reading, or dozing, or just thinking.

He had been lying in bed since ten o'clock. He was not asleep but neither was he properly awake; he lay and reflected—and, it must be confessed, reflected not on official business or on the world at large, but on something that lay close to his heart: on his plan of estate management.
He had left the civil service some years ago, on account of some unpleasantness—a letter sent to the wrong province—and since then had not stirred from his sofa except to go to the window.
He owed a visit to his friend Stoltz, who was always urging him to get up, to get out, to do something. But to get up—to get up from his sofa—this was the central problem of Oblomov's existence.

He would lie and think about what needed to be done—the letter to the estate manager, the reply to his landlord, the plan for improvement of the property at Oblomovka—and think, and think, and then the thoughts would grow pleasantly confused, and he would sleep.
'Zakhar!' he called.
(A shuffling in the next room.)
'Zakhar!'
(More shuffling. A huge, grey-haired man appeared in the doorway.)
ZAKHAR: You called?
OBLOMOV: Did you order the kvass?
ZAKHAR: Yes.
OBLOMOV: Bring it.
(ZAKHAR disappears. OBLOMOV dozes.)

It was past noon when his friend Stoltz appeared. Stoltz was the son of a German father and a Russian mother. He was compact and energetic, with quick eyes and an air of purposeful motion that Oblomov found deeply uncomfortable.
'Still in your dressing-gown, Ilyusha,' said Stoltz.
'I was just about to get up,' said Oblomov.
'You were just about to get up last time I came, and the time before that.'
'I have a plan,' said Oblomov, with sudden animation. 'I am working on a plan for the estate. There are changes to be made. I have it all in my head—'
'Write it down,' said Stoltz.
'I will. Tomorrow.'
Stoltz looked at him with mingled affection and despair.
'Oblomov,' he said, 'you are wasting your life.'
'I am living it,' said Oblomov. 'There is a difference.'

title: Part II: Olga
paragraphs: Stoltz introduced Oblomov to a young woman named Olga Ilyinskaya, and something happened to Oblomov that had not happened to him in many years: he woke up.

Olga was not beautiful in the ordinary sense—she was not pink-cheeked or doll-like. She had a straight, expressive nose and grey eyes that saw everything clearly and did not forgive much. She sang beautifully, with a voice that went directly to the heart.

When Oblomov first heard her sing—heard her voice rise and fill the room with something that was halfway between sorrow and joy—he felt something in his chest that he had thought was entirely extinguished.
'She sang Casta diva,' he told Stoltz afterward. 'And I—I felt—I don't know what I felt. Something I had forgotten.'
'Go and see her again,' said Stoltz.
He went. He kept going back. He sat in the garden and talked with Olga, and she looked at him with those clear grey eyes, and he felt himself becoming alive.

She gave him books to read—books he had not touched in years. She made him get up in the morning and walk. She talked to him about his estates, about his plans, about his life, with an earnest directness that he had never encountered in a woman.
'You could do so much,' she told him. 'You have a good mind. You are kind. Why do you waste yourself?'
'It is not waste,' he said. 'It is peace.'
'It is sleep,' she said. 'There is a difference.'

He fell in love with her. He knew he was in love because when he was with her he forgot to be comfortable. He forgot to think about his sofa and his dressing-gown and the plan for the estate that never got written. He thought only of her.
He told her he loved her, one afternoon in the garden. She looked at him for a long moment.
'What do you mean by love?' she asked.
'I mean—I want to be with you always. I want to—I want to be different, for you. I want to be the man I could have been.'
'Could have been,' she repeated quietly.
'Could be,' he corrected. 'Could be. I will change. I swear to you, Olga, I will change.'

She looked at him with those eyes that forgave nothing, and he saw that she believed him. She believed him because she wanted to believe him, because she too felt something, and because she was young enough still to think that love could make a man into whatever you needed him to be.
'Very well,' she said. 'Then let us begin.'

title: Part III: The Decline
paragraphs: He tried. He truly tried.

He rose earlier. He answered his correspondence—some of it. He made arrangements about the estate—preliminary arrangements. He walked with Olga in the mornings and sat with her in the evenings, and during those hours he was almost the man he had promised to be.

But there were other hours—hours alone in his room—when the old heaviness descended. When the sofa called to him and the dressing-gown awaited him and the thought of all that needed to be done pressed on him like a physical weight until he could not move.

The engagement stretched on. There were practical difficulties—the estate, the finances, a new apartment to be arranged. Each difficulty required action, and action required getting up, and getting up—was difficult.

Olga grew quieter. Her questions became fewer. She watched him with those clear eyes and saw what was happening, and he saw that she saw, and felt ashamed, and the shame too was a weight that kept him down.
One afternoon she came to him and said: 'Ilyusha. I want to ask you something honestly.'
'Ask,' he said.
'Do you love me?'
'Yes,' he said. 'You know I do.'
'Then why are you—' She stopped. She tried again. 'You are becoming what you were before. I can see it happening. Week by week, you are—sinking back.'
He could not deny it.
'I will—' he began.

'You will try again,' she said, gently. 'I know. I believe you will try. But, Ilyusha—I think—' She was quiet for a long moment. 'I think I made a mistake. I thought that love was enough to change a person. I thought love could—could wake someone up and keep them awake. But now I think—I think that a person can only change themselves. Love can open a door but it cannot walk through it for you.'
He sat very still.
'You are ending the engagement,' he said.
She did not deny it.
He did not argue. He did not weep or rage or plead. He sat and felt the familiar heaviness settle over him, and somewhere in its weight was grief, and somewhere was something almost like relief.
'You will be happy,' he said. 'You deserve to be happy.'
'So do you,' she said. And she left.

title: Part IV: Oblomovka
paragraphs: After Olga left, Oblomov moved to a new lodging in the Vyborg Quarter, in the house of a widow named Agafya Matveyevna Pshenitsyna.

It was exactly the sort of house that suited him. It was warm and quiet. Agafya Matveyevna was a large, soft, simple-hearted woman who asked nothing of him but his contentment. She cooked for him and cleaned for him and thought him a great man because he was gentle and seemed to read books sometimes. She did not expect him to rise early or to plan or to change. She expected only that he should eat well and sleep well and be at home when she brought him his tea.
He was at home. He was always at home.

His estates at Oblomovka—the real ones, not the plan that had never been written—began slowly to go to ruin, managed from afar by an agent who had been cheating him for years. Stoltz came and tried to help, to untangle the finances. He looked at Oblomov on his sofa and shook his head.
'Ilyusha,' he said. 'This is Oblomovism.'
'What?'

'Oblomovism. The disease of lying down. Of letting life happen around you and never to you. I have been watching it take you for years and I did not know what to call it, but now I have a name for it. Oblomovism.'
'You make it sound like a sickness,' said Oblomov.
'Is it not?'
Oblomov thought about it.
'Perhaps,' he said. 'Perhaps it is. But there are worse sicknesses. I am not cruel. I am not ambitious. I am not busy destroying anyone's happiness. I lie here and I am—at peace.'
'At peace,' said Stoltz. 'Yes. And the estate?'
'The estate will manage itself,' said Oblomov.

Stoltz married Olga. They went to live abroad, and then came back, and had children, and built a life together. Stoltz was proud of the life they built. He showed it to Oblomov once, when he came to visit.
'This,' said Stoltz, 'is what you could have had.'
Oblomov looked at the house, the garden, the children, Olga's face beside Stoltz, calm and content.
'Perhaps,' he said. 'But perhaps it would not have suited me.'

He married Agafya Matveyevna quietly, without announcement, and lived on in the Vyborg Quarter, and ate her soup and drank his tea and dozed on his sofa. He grew heavier and slower. He had a son, whom he loved very much.
He died one morning without fuss, having simply not woken up.
Stoltz came and looked at him and stood for a long time beside his bed.
'Oblomov,' he said quietly. 'Oblomovism.'
But whether he said it with sorrow or with judgment or with something more complicated than either—that was not clear. Perhaps even Stoltz, who had a name for everything, did not quite know.
